title: The Morning Commute
introduction: The silent hum of electric trams against the roar of the marginal highway. How we move through a city defines who we are within it.

left.title: The Dutch Flow
left.subtitle: Controlled Friction
left.description: 20-minute silent bike rides through engineered calm
left.body:
# The Dutch Flow

In Rotterdam, the morning commute is an act of solitary athleticism. You clip your panniers onto the rear rack, step out into grey-white winter light, and join the invisible infrastructure that holds this city together: the fietspaden.

The bike lanes are not a courtesy here. They are the primary circulatory system. They are separated from car traffic by curb height, by bollards, by decades of political will.

right.title: The Paulista Chaos
right.subtitle: Social Friction
right.body:
# The Paulista Chaos

In São Paulo, the morning commute is a collective negotiation with entropy. You leave your apartment at 6:45 knowing that if you leave at 7:15, the journey will take twice as long.

title: Elevated Highways
introduction: What do you do with a highway that bisects the city but nobody wants to tear down? Two cities found different answers — one still arguing, one already eating brunch on the roof.

left.title: The Viaduct Reborn
left.subtitle: Infrastructure as Amenity
left.description: A 1908 railway viaduct permanently converted into market halls, studios, and a rooftop farm
left.body:
# The Viaduct Reborn

The Hofbogen is easy to miss if you're not looking for it. It doesn't announce itself. It runs for 1.9 kilometres through the Agniesebuurt and Bergpolder neighbourhoods of northern Rotterdam.

right.title: The Minhocão
right.subtitle: Infrastructure as Battleground
right.description: A 3.5 km elevated expressway turned weekend park, permanent closure still under debate
right.body:
# The Minhocão

The Elevado Presidente João Goulart — universally known as the Minhocão, the Big Worm — was built in 1971 as a solution to São Paulo's traffic problem. It is still a highway on weekdays. It is still a park on weekends.

title: Public Markets
introduction: Every city has a room where the abundance of the earth is put on display. The question is whether that room is built for the vendors or for the spectators.

left.title: The Markthal
left.subtitle: Architecture as Market
left.description: A MVRDV-designed horseshoe arch of apartments enclosing Europe's largest indoor market
left.body:
# The Markthal

The Markthal is Rotterdam's most photographed building. Completed by MVRDV in 2014, it is shaped like a horseshoe with 228 residential apartments stacked along the rising flanks and an 11,000 m² mural overhead.

right.title: The Mercadão
right.subtitle: Market as Architecture
right.description: A 1933 neoclassical municipal market where the mortadella sandwich is a civic institution
right.body:
# The Mercadão

The Mercado Municipal de São Paulo has been in operation since 1933. It occupies a neoclassical building on the edge of the Centro Histórico, and it smells of dried cod and ripe mango and years.
